Well, fair enough. Um, look, I, I'm grateful for the opportunity to share,
um, all the good news, uh, about what is being achieved in
education. I'm specifically responsible for attendance, school
lunches, ECE, charter schools, uh, private schools and

Uh, first of all, in attendance, we have recontracted the attendance services, put
in place attendance action plans, implemented the Stepped Attendance Response
scheme, and altogether, we have seen continuously
increasing, uh, attendance. It's been particularly heartening to see the
commitment that Kiwis have to getting to school.
Uh, every t- day this term, we've seen
greater attendance, uh, than the attendance the year
before, uh, with the exception of about three days surrounding
King's birthday. And the reason I say that is it's significant given the
challenges that people have been facing with fuel costs, still getting their, uh,

additional spending that has gone into the attendance service.
Uh, on school lunches, we continue to see, uh, incredible
savings, $122 million saved, uh, versus
the old scheme. Uh, but the savings are actually much greater
because that's the old scheme without adding inflation.
Uh, we've done that bringing on board a whole lot of new contractors this
year, and we have maintained comparable levels of
surplus to the old scheme. The difference is that those young people
will now graduate and have far less government debt to
pay than they would have had we continued with the old scheme.

Could, could we have that information if you don't have that with you now?

That's great if we can have that committee meeting.
That's good. Um, exactly how many students are currently attending charter schools?
Uh, around about 1,600.
Okay. And how many charter schools are there in total right now?
Uh, there must be around about 16, I would say.
So how much has been s- spent since the reintroduction of charter schools
on charter schools?
Mm. Uh-

misleading attempt, uh, to try and make the setup costs,
uh-
Mm

"Well, how long is the restaurant gonna be around for?
How many pizzas is it going to serve per day in the future?" And then

Uh, I think the way that people have tried to misrepresent,
uh, the early days of charter schools when, of course, like any

Are we able to have that information-

So again, I'm happy to help the
member use the
internet. But
in terms of our children pizzas, they're not.
What I was using is an analogy

That's nice.
How much on average has been spent on each charter school since they were

students. And that-

Yes, it's slightly less than is spent on a student in a state

In each case, it's about $400 less per year per student.
That is the true comparison over the 10 years that they're contracted

Right.
And any attempts to provide cherry-picked or misleading
figures,

But I want to move on and ask the minister-

And you persist in trying to-- And this is why I gave you the

We don't-

Great. Give me a range as of last week.

Right.

state school would've got for teaching the same student-

... and asking them to get the outcomes, and the outcomes are charter school

educators have more time to spend on students, and therefore you will

Mark would.
But that's not normal.

So-
Well, some would use other expressions-

The
other thing I would say that to the extent that you believe that class

ability to lessen their class sizes despite having the same funding.
And the reason for that is that they are able

So by redirecting their funding to get smaller sizes, it's very useful.
Another thing they can do, one charter school in the past actually had

not for the faint-hearted.
Mm.
And if all you have in training is a bunch of Marxist claptrap that you got from

And so this kind of on-the-job training is very helpful.
Okay. Supplementary question from Carl Bates.
Thanks, Chair. Thanks, Minister.
So talking about the actual outcomes for students, which is
a novel concept for some-
... can you tell us about the results that charter schools
have delivered for student achievement-
Mm
... for the students actually attending these schools?
Yeah. So first of all, let me just talk a little bit about being there.
Mm.
So across the board,
we've only got results for the seven that opened in early 2025.
The others, it's too early to say. But in the ones that have completed a full
year, five out of the seven have exceeded their targets, and
most of the five have actually exceeded the government's goal of 80%
regular attendance.
So that's been a spectacular thing.
The two that haven't are schools that were deliberately
set up for students who are completely disengaged.
One of them, Christchurch North College, set up by four state school principals
because they knew in their heart of hearts there were students enrolled at their
school they couldn't reach. And unlike many in the state sector who are hostile
to charter schools, they actually decided to embrace the opportunity to
give kids greater educational opportunity.
So they did this. But of course, that school, every term, new
students enroll who are not in the habit of going to school.
So even though they've made great progress with individual students in the first
year, their average has been weighed down by inducting those new
students each term. But over time, they'll make more progress.
So that's achieved engagement or attendance.
On achievement, we have set
similar targets that effectively mean they have to do as well or better
than equivalent state schools. And again, five of
the seven have exceeded their targets, have effectively done better than
equivalent state schools at a similar level of EQI.
Those that haven't, one of them I point to would be Mastery, which I've just
discussed. Mastery was specifically set up
to take children who were having serious learning difficulties,
dyslexia or other similar diagnosed
conditions. 60% of students at Mastery have such a
condition. So in their first year, they didn't make their target.
But what will happen, and what has happened, in fact, is that the authorization
board, which is made of student educators, Dame
Professor Elizabeth Rata,
Justine Mann, former
principal of St. Cuthbert's College, they will be assessing the
schools and saying, "You missed your target.
What's your plan to get back?" And assessing whether they need
further sanctions. We don't performance manage schools like
that anywhere else in the sector. But in return for having all the
flexibility we talked about, charter schools have that extra accountability.
Thank you. Supplementary question from Parmjeet Parmar.
Thank you. Minister, you released figures about setting up a new charter school for
100 students, which was
$401,675, and compare that
with setting up a new state school, and the cost, as
per your release, was $638,249.
Can you tell us how is it that charter schools are saving so much money for

Yeah. So that figure you're quoting is the cost of starting
up a new school. If you have a state school, for example, a new
state school, they will actually pay a principal for five terms
prior to establishment. With a charter school, we pay them for one term.
And there's several differences like that where we just don't give as much
money to a new charter school as we would to a new state school.
And then, of course, there's also the operating cost,
where because state schools frequently
get increases in some parts of their funding formula,
they are getting these uplifts that don't always apply to charters.
So as a result, charter schools get slightly less per student
in operating funding.
But we think the flexibility allows them to still outperform.
In fact, we've shown that the flexibility they have allows them to outperform
state schools.
Right. For the supplementary, yeah?
Yeah. So this is about the enrollment demand that, Minister, you mentioned that
it's increasing quite significantly for charter schools.
Mm.
So in the funding that is there for this budget year-
Mm
... are you sure that there is enough to meet the demand?
Well, there will be because the primary and secondary funding
appropriation can be accessed as students shift
from the state or integrated system into a
charter school. That funding follows them.
So, there will be enough funding. They can grow almost infinitely.
And I think it's a wonderful opportunity.
There's been several examples already where charter
schools have actually had to move premises
because there is so much demand.
That's great.
I was speaking to somebody,

Great.
